definition: A candlestick pattern formed by a bullish sequence of low, high, lower low, then higher high, or a bearish sequence of high, low, higher high, then lower low. The series of up-close (bullish) or down-close (bearish) candles between the initial swing low and high becomes the breaker block, expected to act as support or resistance when price retests it after a liquidity sweep and structural break.
identification_rules: Identify the swing sequence: bullish: low, high, lower low, higher high; bearish: high, low, higher high, lower low. All four points must be confirmed before marking.
Mark the series of up-close candles (bullish) or down-close candles (bearish) between the initial swing low and high as the breaker zone, using the full candle range including wicks.
If candle bodies are solid with small wicks, use the body and its 50% average threshold; if wicks are large, use the wick-to-close range and 0.5 Fibonacci level.

Confirm a prior liquidity sweep (stop raid) of the first extreme followed by displacement closing beyond the opposing candle series and the swept high/low, validating the structural break (change in state of delivery).
If no liquidity sweep occurs, classify as Mitigation Block instead of Breaker Block.
Wait for price to retest the breaker zone; on retest, it should hold as support/resistance without closing bodies through the mean threshold (0.5 Fibonacci) of the breaker candles.
Optionally, if the swing point is not swept but an SMT divergence is present, the swing can be considered protected and the breaker block still valid.
Look for nested confluences such as fair value gaps, order blocks, or OTE retracements within the breaker zone for higher-probability entries.
If the higher timeframe shows a single candle, use multiple candles on lower timeframe to mark the zone.
trading_implication: Breaker blocks act as high-probability entry zones upon retest after a liquidity sweep and structural break. Price is expected to reject the breaker and continue in the direction of the displacement. Entries are refined by overlapping with fair value gaps, order blocks, or OTE retracements (Unicorn model). Stops are placed just beyond the breaker's range or high resistance liquidity; targets include opposing liquidity, swing points, or 2R+ projections. The setup is used within a top-down framework aligned with premium/discount zones and killzone timing.
common_mistakes: Taking a breaker entry without a prior liquidity sweep and confirmed displacement/structural shift.

Misidentifying the pattern by calling a formation a breaker when the required lower low/higher high sequence is not fully formed, or confusing it with a simple swing reversal, mitigation block, or order block.
Incorrectly marking the breaker zone by using only the opening price (like an order block) instead of the full candle range, or including all candles instead of only up-close/down-close ones.
Entering a breaker when price is in the premium (for longs) or discount (for shorts) of the displacement range.
Placing the stop loss at the far swing high/low instead of just beyond the breaker, leading to poor risk-reward, or failing to adjust stops during high-volatility events.
Ignoring the mean threshold (0.5 Fib) of the breaker candles, allowing a close through bodies to invalidate the zone.
Entering the retest prematurely before price reaches the breaker, or without higher timeframe bias and PD array confluence.
Overcomplicating the analysis by using too many PD arrays simultaneously instead of focusing on a single, consistent entry model.
Assuming every breaker block will trigger a reversal; reactions are not guaranteed.
Confusing Breaker Block with Mitigation Block: Breaker requires liquidity sweep, Mitigation only requires the four-point structure.
Ignoring multi-timeframe nesting and killzone restrictions.

related_concepts.term: Change in the state of delivery

merge_notes: All four input cards are from ICT school except one SMC card; majority ICT. SMC card had simpler rules (no liquidity sweep requirement) but was superseded by more detailed ICT rules. No fundamental disagreement on definition; SMC card lacked identification details. All rules and mistakes from ICT cards were merged; SMC card's two mistakes were already covered.
